speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: I also-- during the war, I had one dream, a recurring dream. The dream only stopped when I was deported. It was a dream there's a big bridge with the Amstel-- River Amstel in Amsterdam. And that occurs when I'm in the middle-- lots of people. When I'm in the middle of the bridge, the bridge opens, and I fall in the water. But before reaching, touching the water, I wake up. And this was a recurring dream quite often before I was deported. Afterwards, when I was in the camp, I had no such dream. And this means that the dream is-- was very vivid. I still remember that's exactly what happened. I mentioned the first time that my father saved my life. That was when he pulled me over the drawbridge. The second time was when there was a market, and-- like you have the markets here with groceries, but it was only to be frequented by Jews. So at that time we had to buy some groceries, and I was hungry. I said-- my father said, OK, I go with you. We went on the market. We did some shopping. And then all of a sudden, we heard the motorcycles and the cars coming. And we knew there was another round-up, because the market was full of Jews, Jewish people who bought their groceries. And that was the exact spot for, again, mice or rat in a fault caught in a trap. My father pulled me out. We went just by the exit. And when we rounded the corner, the Germans on the motorcycle rounded the corner. So my father turned us around so that they looked at our backs and couldn't see our stars, which we were wearing. Then they entered the market, and as fast as we could, we walked over to one of the houses where we're lucky enough to know-- to have some friends there. There, from the window of those friend's house or flat, we watched the horror, how they were beating and taking the people into trucks and rounding the whole market up. And actually we were shaken. It took us quite a while to-- for everything was safe, so we could leave for home. But in the meantime, my mother was home. And everybody heard about the round-up. And she was semi-conscious when we came home, because in that time, as I said, we had no phone. And there was nothing to tell her that we were safe. When we came home, she was beside herself. And it took quite a time for her to come back, to get her composure. That was the second time that my father saved my life. Then also in 19-- yeah, it was 1941 or 1942, my mother was still alive. There was a night of bombing, English coming over. And I don't know if it was accidentally or what, there was a bomb falling on our neighbor's-- a couple of houses [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] distance from us. And in that house, [LAUGHS] watching, naturally, families, but there was a family, family Bromet. And I played with the guy and his brother. And his name was Harry Bromet. And he was born on the same date, the same year as my husband, who is also Harry Bromet, which is a very coincidence. That boy was killed at that night. That is one of the freak things from life that later on I happen to marry Harry Bromet. It's the same thing. He will tell the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the night of the bomb. It was a horrible night. We were standing in the hall and we were-- didn't know if it would hit us. Then I know we were outside on the street, and I remember I pulled my mother down. There was grass on-- when a flower-- we were flat on the grass. I had to, because we heard the, the anti-aircraft. And it was a night of horror.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we, we-- during the middle of the night, we had friends living a little bit distance from us. And we walked over there, and we spent the rest of the night there. And the next morning we returned home. That was that night. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then that was what I want to tell, the second time that my father saved my life. And that was the incidents of fate that I had to-- that Harry Bromet was killed, my-- the playmate. Life went on till-- I would say till 1942 in August, as I said that my mother died at that time. And we lived in the house. My father at that time had a kind of an ID from the Germans that he was needed for the war effort in his diamond-- whatever he did. So we were saved for further round-ups or deportation. And we know that was a very temporary thing, because whatever the whim of the Germans was if they wanted to revoke it, they did. And on one faithful-- fateful night on February-- in February 1943, my-- they came to our flat and took us out of our house, my father-- my father and I. My grandfather was sleeping in the little room just beside the living room. We thought we will not tell the ones who picked us up that there was another one living. But at the last moment, if you're ready to go downstairs, they discovered that my grandfather was still sleeping. So they had to hurry the poor guy out of his bed, and hurry, hurry, thrashing. And they were very, very angry that we tied to hide somebody to stay in the house. We were led to a shelter somewhere in Amsterdam, in the south close to us. And it was an air raid shelter. There they held us till there was transportation. And we were transported to that theater in Amsterdam, in the center of Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were held with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole theater was always full.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I mean, I assume yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All from Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, mhm. Because in the middle of Amsterdam-- yes. It was an, an, an-- a horror, things that are now monuments. And part of the front and the back of that theater is-- remains still. I've been there. And all the names from people who moved in there and were there is all memorialized in the-- in the theater now. They kept the front and the back of the building.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the theater for?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then during the night-- we, my grandfather, father, and I-- and then we were outside. We were-- had to go outside, and like cattle. And we had-- there was a streetcar waiting. And the streetcar brought us, in the middle of the night, to the train station. And the train station, they brought us to the camp. It was called at that time a labor camp, Vught, V-U-G-H-T. It was in February '43. In Vught, there was-- there was first and-- prisoners of war were kept there. And there were still a few there, present. The one horrifying thing I remembered is standing in front of the window there in one of the barracks, and seeing one shot and being, being-- the body being carried away in a wheelbarrow, just from there. That was-- one still from the prisoners of war Then there was-- my father went to the barracks of the men. My grandfather was sick and came in the sick barrack. My grandfather didn't stay too long. He was too sick. He was deported with other sick people to Westerbork, the camp in the northern part of Holland. And I heard later on he was-- he died there and was cremated there. There was a crematorium in [INAUDIBLE], Westerbork. Not to be used what they did in Birkenau, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first days in Vught like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- don't remember too much. It was just-- it was-- food was nil. It was some hot water with some cold [INAUDIBLE] in, in-- floating in there, a very, very, very not enough. But every day came more and more people-- were-- came into the camp. There was-- my father, as I said, was in the men's barrack. He later on started working in the kind of wood factory what they had. There were other-- not all, all Jewish. There were German war criminals, communist men there. Women, I don't know. But they were there. Because they were all signified-- they had a little round-- denotes the color what you were. Red was a criminal. The other was a homosexual. There was that, and they-- they had those, the men there too in the men's barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father started working there and there was also a kind of a seamstress. There's a fur-- little fur industry. And there was-- and, and you could-- and sewing factory was there, too. And we do-- and some men were working outside Vught, in a town, to do with heavy construction. So they were out of the camp and they came back. And where were you housed?

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the mean-- I was with the women's barrack. I-- it was a [INAUDIBLE] thing. They tried to, to let me work in the fur industry, but I didn't do anything. I just must have floated around.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the daily routine like?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is all-- that, I don't know. There were lots of babies in that-- in that camp. There were also two women-- how you call them? Women who are supervising us and [GERMAN], you call them. The one who--

speaker: interviewer
text: Supervised--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, supervised and tried to do tricks with us. We had to stand long, long hours on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Appell.

speaker: interviewee
text: Appell, yeah. Is there an English word for it? I don't know. And long hours there, and they tried to play tricks on us. One sometimes said, now you have to stand and do like the dogs pee. Every women had to start with their leg like that. You know, they had a sadistic pleasure in that. And they, they, they, they had-- they had fun with us. But then the Philips-- in the meantime, Philips was starting with the little factory-- with a factory. I didn't know it at that time. Then as I said, there were many, many, many babies there. And it was too much. The babies were apparently not treated too well. And there were needed too many people and nurses, so they decided the camp has to be trimmed. And all-- in June '43, the people-- the kids from babies till-- until 16 years had to be deported, and they had to have one parent with them. And that was the third time, and the last time my father saved my life, because at the time I was 15 years old. And I had to go. My father took me outside of the camp at that time that that day was on. He talked with one of the officers. I didn't see him. I had to wait outside while I was witness to lots of women and women on stretchers who had just given birth. Baby-- in the little-- premature babies in the little basket were outside to be hauled onto buses. Before that, I-- just a short while before that happened, I met a relative of mine. And it was an aunt. It was a relative of my mother. She was there with her two children. And by coincidence, I met her because her name was van Bingen, and that was my mother's maiden name. So I was quite happy to have a relative there. At that time, the coincidence how I met her-- at first-- at that time still for me in the camp, and a little bit embarrassing-- I just caught lice, head lice. And it was horrible. And she just met me when I had one of those things on my head, to, to get rid of the lice. That just was an incident, because of the most time that you want to have a relative, I met her. And it was nice to have her, although for a brief time. And what I remember always-- because I was waiting for the officers of the SS [GERMAN] outside the camp, close. And she was-- came out with her two children, and she called me to join her in the bus. And I said, no I can't. I have to wait for my father. If I would have listened to her-- no, I didn't. I said, no, my father is here. I have to wait until he comes back. And that was the last I saw of her, and later on I heard that the children, and the mothers, and all them, they were transported to Sobibor. And the rest is, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then at that time, they also had too much people beside when the children were gone, the people who worked in the-- in this-- in the [NON-ENGLISH]. A seamstress and the fur industry, they were all abandoned, disbanded. They-- all those people who worked there were also being deported to Westerbork.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody went by the train to the northern part, because Westerbork was the, the gateway to hell, you can call it. From the gateway from Westerbork, they went deported straight to Auschwitz. And so those people were deported, too. In the meantime, there was, like you say, for employment by the Philips. Philips choose people to be working in their little factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they choose--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, choose-- they told people who are a little bit in the-- in the commun-- in the-- men or so, you are. And there probably people also from Eindhoven. We call them civilist-- civil person, because they were not like us. They were working for Philips, but they were coming daily, were leaving for home in Eindhoven.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they choose people if you had good eyesight or because for the special work which you were doing-- supposed to do. I was chosen, too, although my eyesight was not so hot at that-- for far. They choose me and quite a few people, too. How is it that they chose you? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to do a little examination with the eye, look here and there. But I mean, they knew that-- because one of our own had to choose, so they choose as much-- as many people as possible, actually. [LAUGHS] So then the-- we started working there. And then before that I started working there, my father, who was at that time very ill. It was under-nourishment. He was-- his face, his legs, his whole body was swollen from edema, too much water and, and, and the bad food, because we never received any, any, any parcels. We had nobody else. Some people were there still received Red Cross parcels. They were sometimes allowed to bring through. Sometimes the Germans and the SS took them and ate them. My father-- it was and-- on round, on appell, and then-- there were people who were just going a transport to Germany, and they eliminated the people who were sick at that time. They choose them all, because they were no good. They couldn't work. And there was one incident that-- I don't know, my father, why he had to come forward in the middle. But all the people were standing there on appell. And apparently whatever they asked him, well, the answer was not satisfactory. And then one of the SS, he kicked him, and he fell on the floor. If you can describe that thing, if you see that somebody's kicking your father and you can't do anything about, and he was already sick. The next day that transport was to be gone. He managed to come to the women's quarters on the outside. It was all barbed wire. And the last I got, he said goodbye. And I got a kiss through the barbed wire from him, and that was it. That was my father. But he, that third time, he was able to save my life. Also, on the other side when some, some comic things, more or less. It was still-- there was some theater, and some entertainment was sometimes going on in Vught. And I don't think I attended that, but I heard about it. Kids were singing and-- at that time, and the kids were still there. And there are some songs that sometimes they sing, and if I hear them, I cringe. I remember, though, those kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: There also, the funny part, the comic part is that they had put on the barbed wire bottles, and glass, and bells. So why? Because still, although they put some stuff in the food that inhibits the, the-- Sexual desire.

speaker: interviewee
text: --sexual desires, it didn't work so apparently for some men. And they tried over to climb the barbed wire, and went into the women's barracks. So when you knew that-- I mean, I was a child still, but still, when you heard the clinking, you knew that someone's trying to get in. I think quite a couple succeeded, I think. That was the semi-comic part of, of that. And on Sundays we were allowed visits in the beginning, from the men visited the children, or the husbands visited the wives. That was still allowed in the beginning. Now then I started to work with Philips, and it was come some [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] flashlights, but primitive flashlights, what you move with your hand, and then that produces the lights. No clue how it is in English. But that's what I-- that they made on the assembly line. And I was soldering something for the fluorescent lights for transformers for the fluorescent lights in the airplanes. That's what they said. I liked the work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was at a machine and I was soldering. And I, I, I didn't mind it. I liked that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were connecting metal together with heat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, soldering. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I have still something here that I had dropped it on my finger, and it was hot. And there was also issued food, they called the Philips, [INAUDIBLE] Philips food. But special food, what you used to have at home, they were allowed in big, big vats, or to transport it from Eindhoven to the camp. And especially the children, the young people were twice or three times a week-- were given that extra food, in addition to the lousy camp food what we had anyway. Whatever it was, it was-- it's not enough. So that went on to June 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the work for Philips in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was in the camp. That was definitely in the camp. Yes, we were in barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was supervising the workers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, must be somebody. Germans were-- Germans were around there, too, naturally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have somebody making sure you were working properly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe, yes. That could be, naturally. I assume it would be. Then without an eventful-- yeah, what I also, what also happened during that time is that they-- we had sometimes to undress and to-- well, that was their pleasure or whatever, to undress and to go to see if you're all healthy and you had no lice. And that also happened. Otherwise, as I can recall, we went on our way to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there-- excuse me Leny. Were there times when the Germans threatened the operation of the Philips workers, perhaps where the Germans wanted to take the Philips workers away?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not yet. Not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: That occurred only later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it occurred later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I try to think, but I-- at the moment, I don't have anymore recollection of that. It just more or less, how do you say, in a normal working trend, I realized that in the meantime before that people were deported from Vught to Westerbork. But that was before the Philips. Then there was just the Philips Group together men. And women, and non-Jewish men, non-Jewish women, they were working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You call it the Phillips Group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm. I think I read it. I had a newspaper what was a [INAUDIBLE]. I think it was maybe 200. I don't know exactly. I don't know. Just don't forget, I know I'm recollecting when I was 15 years old. I don't know how many people. Maybe 200, maybe a hundred. There was-- it could be 200, because men and women, Jewish men and women were working, non-Jews too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were all the Jewish people from Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, oh yeah, yeah, yeah. Yeah. Yeah, yeah. Then in June '44, the invasion started. And the-- I think they wanted to eliminated the camp. So the Germans took us to the train, tried to. It was during the day. And during the day, the Philips was working. And Philips executives, how you call it, were there, too, from Eindhoven. And one way or another they got word that we were about to be deported to Germany, wherever at that time. We don't know. So they thwarted the effort of the Germans. And whatever they did, the Germans put us back in the barracks, and for the next-- I don't know how long, a week, two weeks, week and a half, we were working. And then in the middle of the night, they took us from our bed, hopped into the train, and over the border. We heard later on that-- even while we were on the train we heard that-- you know rumors fly always-- that in cattle cars we were deported to, and we ended up in Auschwitz-Birkenau. The Philips tried desperately to save us. And they even sent a cable to Auschwitz, but we were in the middle of Germany. It was not-- they were not-- they were unable to, to save us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the train went on, and this was the Jewish part of the Philips Group. The non-Jewish part of the Philips Group was-- and the women were deported in September 1943, and they were sent to Ravensbruck. It's also well known camp, R-A-V-E-N-S-B-R-U-C-K. It's for the non-Jewish women from Philips in September. Then the camp was no camp anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived during the night in Auschwitz-Birkenau. A commanding officer was standing while I was on the platform. We had some small children with us from Philips, but we all remained in one group. He said, this is the Philips Group from Eindhoven. He said, now we'll name it from Eindhoven. We were all headed into shower rooms. And there we had to take our clothes off and remain there the whole night without any clothing, without food naturally, nothing. The next morning the inmates came and told us what kind of a camp Birkenau-Auschwitz was, what was going on, and crematoria, and so on. Then we were all tattooed. We all got a number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe what you were being told?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did it make you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. We were all shocked anyway. That-- you, you were more or less an automaton. You-- it's, it's-- I don't know if I can recall any, any, any, any, any thought. You were scared, yes, but you behaved with group. You were not an individual, and that was the purpose of it, to deaden you. You were not an individual anymore too much. I mean, I was not. I was just 16 at that time. Right?

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were tattooed. The woman made a little error there, I think. And she did it over again. And the children who were there from Philips, they were tattooed under their arms, in the-- in the-- in the inside. And remaining-- the children remained in Auschwitz till the end, because it was the Philips Group. Because apparently we were considered, we were a labor force and of use to the Germans. That's why they didn't do with us what they did with the other people. The--

speaker: interviewee
text: Part of our group remained in Birkenau for four days. After four days, that part of the group left. And our group, including me, the rest remained in Auschwitz. And we were scared, because we thought we knew what was going to happen, and we saw it, and that is going to happen to us. And we, as far as I can recall, we didn't-- we stood on appell day and night, and whatever they wanted to, cold, and hungry, and ever. It was horrible. We had to do our business sometimes in the wheelbarrow, and that's it. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH] I remember one day that-- the only day I worked and that was they told us to carry bricks from someplace to the railroad. On that day, I was very close to the crematorium and to the tower. And when the railroad arriving-- and it was in the summer, a Hungarian transport with people. And there I saw with my own eyes the selection was going on. I saw the women with young children, the elderly people, other people, I saw them being selected. And later on, the smoke went up. That was a day if there wouldn't have been a barrier between on the railroad, I would have gone, too. I would have joined the Hungarians. That was my feeling.
